document.source_title: Clinical trial finds no difference in fluid treatment options for pediatric sepsis

claims.claim_text: The trial found no difference in meaningful outcomes between two fluid treatment options for pediatric sepsis.
claims.evidence_text: A large clinical trial supported by the National Institutes of Health (NIH) compared two commonly used treatments for pediatric patients treated for septic shock and found no difference in meaningful outcomes.

entities.name: National Institutes of Health
entities.description: The nation's medical research agency, which supported the study.
entities.evidence_text: A large clinical trial supported by the National Institutes of Health (NIH) compared two commonly used treatments for pediatric patients treated for septic shock.

entities.name: Pediatric Emergency Care Applied Research Network
entities.description: A network for research in pediatric emergency medicine that led the study.
entities.evidence_text: This research was supported by NIH grants R01HD101528 and P50DK114786 and led by the Pediatric Emergency Care Applied Research Network (PECARN).

events.evidence_text: Clinical trial finds no difference in fluid treatment options for pediatric sepsis

metrics.name: participants
metrics.evidence_text: The trial, which enrolled over 9,000 participants across five countries.

metrics.name: hospital-free days
metrics.evidence_text: Participants in both groups received their assigned IV fluid type for 24-48 hours and achieved a median of 23 hospital-free days during the 28 days following enrollment.

citations.title: Clinical trial finds no difference in fluid treatment options for pediatric sepsis

source_credibility.reason: The study was supported by the National Institutes of Health, a reputable medical research agency.
